ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers steamed bread/bun category; no specific code exists for Singaporean pork-filled pau. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large steamed pork pau typically weighs approximately 150 g and constitutes a single serving as a breakfast or snack item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Big Pau (Pork) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Big Pau (Pork), cr_id F05020011, with confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are preserved verbatim. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun / Chinese steamed bun — analogous GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat-flour steamed buns
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean Big Pau (Pork) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: steamed wheat-flour bun (Chinese mantou-style), which has a reported GI of approximately 65 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The pork and egg filling does not materially alter the GI of the bun component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 42.2) / 100 ≈ 27.4; where 150 g serving × 28.13 g carbohydrate/100 g = 42.2 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 344 mg/100 g with negligible potassium offset and very low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Big Pau (Pork) to half a portion per meal, avoid additional soy sauce dipping, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load of 516 mg per 150 g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour bun with carbohydrate 28.1 g/100 g, sugar 10.0 g/100 g, and dietary fibre only 0.9 g/100 g driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict Big Pau (Pork) to half a bun per occasion, pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt glycaemic response, and prefer wholegrain pau variants where available given the medium-high GI (estimated 65) and high sugar-to-fibre ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (8.42 g/100 g) from pork filling with likely saturated fat contribution, combined with high sodium (344 mg/100 g) and high sugar (10.0 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Big Pau (Pork) to occasional consumption, as the combination of pork-derived saturated fat, high sodium (516 mg per 150 g serving), and refined carbohydrates with added sugar collectively elevates cardiovascular risk; choosing steamed chicken pau or reducing portion size is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork filling provides arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; refined flour and sugar (10 g/100 g) contribute to glycaemic-driven inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise Big Pau (Pork) due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork and the absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients; steamed chicken or vegetable pau are preferable alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Big Pau (Pork) is a large Singaporean Chinese steamed bun filled with seasoned minced pork and a whole or halved boiled egg. The bun is made from a soft, leavened wheat-flour dough that is steamed until fluffy and slightly glossy on the outside. The filling typically combines minced pork with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and sugar, giving it a savoury-sweet flavour profile. The boiled egg embedded in the filling adds richness and protein. Big Pau is a popular breakfast and snack item sold at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and dim sum stalls across Singapore. Per 100 g, it provides 224 kcal, 9.05 g protein, 8.42 g fat, 28.13 g carbohydrate, 10.03 g sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 344 mg sodium. The high sugar content relative to very low fibre makes it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The pork filling contributes arachidonic acid, which is relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A typical serving is one large pau weighing approximately 150 g, delivering approximately 336 kcal and 516 mg sodium per serving. The food is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets due to pork content, and contains gluten, egg, and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a big pork pau?
How much sodium is in a steamed pork bun?
Is big pau suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020011) with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (steamed wheat-flour bun) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: the HPB Foodo database entry for Big Pau (Pork) reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre; detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and moisture data are not published for this item in the HPB database as of 2026. No secondary approved source (USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact match for this Singaporean preparation. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour bun entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Big Pau (Pork) was found. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (65 × 42.2) / 100 = 27.4. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.429 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this source is not on the approved list and has been replaced with the University of Sydney analogous estimate. Big Pau (Pork) contains pork and is not halal-certified; it is not suitable for Muslim dietary requirements. Allergens present: gluten (wheat flour), egg (boiled egg in filling), soy (soy sauce, oyster sauce).
